interview_metadata.interviewee_title: CORE Field Secretary; Freedom Ride Co-Conceiver; Biology Professor
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Dr. Thomas Walter Gaither recounts his life from a rural South Carolina upbringing through a pivotal role in the nonviolent civil rights movement of the early 1960s and a long subsequent career as a biology professor. Born to Walter B. Gaither and Fannie Mae Little Gaither in Great Falls, South Carolina, he grew up in a household that prized education despite economic hardship; his mother taught him through seven years of a one-room AME Zion church school (Pleasant Grove), while his father, terminated from teaching for questioning a five-dollar payroll discrepancy, later worked as a cotton-mill fireman, brickmason, and Benedict College graduate. Gaither and his brother attended Claflin College in Orangeburg, South Carolina, beginning in 1956, where he became president of the campus NAACP youth chapter and later helped organize sit-ins jointly with South Carolina State students after the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins.

Mentored by CORE field secretary James T. McCain of Sumter, Gaither helped lead the Orangeburg sit-in movement and the mass arrest of roughly 350 demonstrators, met regional NAACP figures including Ruby Hurley and Medgar Evers, and after graduation worked on voter registration in South Carolina with Frank Robinson. In summer 1960 he attended a CORE Action Institute in Miami, where he was arrested in Hollywood, Florida on a charge of "ejection of undesirable guests" alongside Dottie Miller and Bernard Lafayette, after which he was hired by CORE as a field secretary. Gaither trained the Friendship Junior College students in Rock Hill who became the Friendship Nine, deliberately staging their jail-no-bail action on January 31, 1961 to let the students complete semester registration. In December 1960, traveling by bus from South Carolina to New York with Gordon Carey, Gaither participated in the conversation in which the idea of the 1961 Freedom Ride emerged; he then served as the scout for the route through Southern cities and historically black colleges, and was assigned to Montgomery (where he stayed in Ralph Abernathy's home during martial law) and then to Jackson, Mississippi to receive arriving riders.

Gaither describes the philosophical evolution from tactical nonviolence toward the deeper Gandhian commitment he encountered through Glenn Smiley of the Fellowship of Reconciliation and the writings of Louis Fischer, and reflects on his ambivalent assessment of the federal government (critical of the FBI and the Kennedys' political calculations, while crediting John Doar of the Justice Department). After a summer 1963 stint with Aaron E. Henry's NAACP selective-buying boycott in Clarksdale, Mississippi and a Freedom School there, Gaither departed for graduate school at Atlanta University and then the University of Iowa, ultimately earning a PhD in botany with a specialization in electron microscopy, and later joined the faculty at what became Slippery Rock University in Pennsylvania.

Throughout the interview Gaither emphasizes that the Freedom Rides were conceived and primarily executed by CORE, defends CORE's role against revisionist accounts that overstate the Nashville movement's contribution, and offers reflections on the persistent infrastructure of racism, the unfinished work of social change, and the loneliness of being a Black scholar whose specialized work was difficult for his community and even his parents to understand.
key_themes: CORE and the Origins of the 1961 Freedom Rides
Jail-No-Bail Tactic and the Friendship Nine
Nonviolent Direct Action: From Tactic to Philosophy
Rural Black Southern Family Life and Education Under Segregation
Federal Government Ambivalence Toward the Movement
From Movement Work to Academic Career
main_summary.historical_significance: This interview is a primary-source account from one of the principal organizers of two foundational events of the early 1960s movement: the Friendship Nine jail-no-bail action of January 31, 1961 and the 1961 Freedom Rides, which Gaither co-conceived with Gordon Carey and for which he served as scout. Gaither's testimony documents CORE's institutional perspective on the Rides (often subordinated in later narratives to the Nashville continuation) and provides firsthand evidence on the Orangeburg sit-in movement, the role of CORE field secretary James T. McCain in South Carolina, and the Jackson, Mississippi reception logistics during the height of the Freedom Rider arrests. It also contributes a rare academic-trajectory perspective from a Movement organizer who entered the biological sciences.

improvements: The summary attributes the Friendship Nine jail-no-bail action to 'January 31, 1961,' which matches the corpus and Gaither's transcript statement that 'we decided to have the arrest on the 31st' a day before the anniversary date; this is consistent but readers should note Gaither phrases this as a deliberate choice rather than a documentary date stamp.

Summary characterizes the Hollywood, Florida arrest charge as 'ejection of undesirable guests' per Gaither's testimony; this is the exact phrase he uses but the formal Florida statute name is not independently verified in transcript.
Could note explicitly that Gaither describes the cross-burning at his parents' home and the National Guard escort logistics in Montgomery as personal observation.

chapters.title: Family Origins and Father's Termination from Teaching in Great Falls, South Carolina
chapters.summary: Gaither introduces his parents Walter B. Gaither and Fannie Mae Little Gaither, both Friendship Junior College graduates in Rock Hill, South Carolina who became schoolteachers in Great Falls, his father's hometown (his mother was originally from Anderson, South Carolina). He describes how his father was terminated from teaching after he confronted the white school-board voucher official about a five-dollar discrepancy between his contractual pay and his actual paycheck, at a time when boards of education were composed entirely of white men with token Black 'figurehead' trustee boards. After losing his teaching job, his father worked as a fireman/night-watchman in a cotton mill, lost that job as well (which Gaither suspects was due to his progressive views), did handyman and construction work, and eventually returned to Benedict College in Columbia to complete a Bachelor of Science in sociology. A local Baptist minister blocked his father's bid to become principal of the local Black high school, which Gaither in retrospect considers fortunate.
related_events: Great Falls, South Carolina cotton mill

improvements: Could note Gaither's reflection that his father not becoming principal was 'one of the best things' due to community jealousy.

chapters.title: Pleasant Grove One-Room School and the Move to Consolidated Schools
chapters.summary: Gaither describes attending a one-room school at Pleasant Grove AME Zion church, where his mother was the teacher and the state paid the salary while the church provided the building. He recounts the practical conditions: a single large central drum heater that smoked the room when its lid started 'floating up and down,' student water jars labeled with adhesive tape and stored by a back-room cooler, no library, and second-hand textbooks passed down from white students' classrooms. He describes the pedagogical advantage of the multi-grade setup (younger students could overhear higher-grade lessons) and traces his mother's continued teaching career until the 'consolidation of schools' in the early 1950s, when many new 'separate but equal' Black elementary schools were hastily built across South Carolina in apparent anticipation of the 1954 Supreme Court decision. He notes that he eventually attended high school in Chester County (about 2.5 miles away) rather than Fairfield County (Winnsboro, about 17 miles).
related_events: Pleasant Grove AME Zion church school

improvements: Could elaborate on Gaither's first public-speaking memory at Children's Day at Pleasant Grove.

chapters.title: Dinner-Table Politics, the Till Murder, NAACP Support, and Voter Registration
chapters.summary: Gaither describes the political education he received around the family dinner table, where his parents 'shared liberally' their views on race relations. He specifically recalls his father's discussions of the 1955 Emmett Till case as a formative influence on his later movement participation, noting that Till and the Montgomery bus boycott (same year) created cumulative momentum being internalized in his household. He recounts how his father hosted union organizers at the cotton mill, which contributed to his eventual termination, and how his parents were openly supportive of the NAACP despite social cost; Gaither remembers attending state NAACP conference meetings as a child of less than 10. He notes a family tug-of-war between his parents and two aunts (also teachers) over the South Carolina NAACP's suit to equalize teacher salaries, with the aunts opposing the NAACP for 'stirring up' trouble. He describes the National Teacher Examination salary tiering (A/B/C) that kept the best-paid teachers in well-funded districts and left small towns like Great Falls with C-tier teachers. He confirms his parents were registered voters and discusses the certificate of qualified elector with its disqualifying clauses (paupers, stealing chickens, wife-beating, public drunkenness, etc.) and the South Carolina Constitution interpretation requirement.

improvements: Could quote Gaither's specific contemporary parallel about voter-ID law resurgence as a 'throwback' to disqualification.

chapters.title: Claflin College, the GRE Off-Campus Incident, and Father's Clemson Allegiance
chapters.summary: Gaither describes choosing Claflin College in Orangeburg with his brother (he as valedictorian, brother as salutatorian, with $75 and $50 scholarships respectively), and acknowledges that for a Black student in the 1950s the choice was confined to Black colleges (Allen, Benedict, Morris, or Claflin). He recounts a pivotal incident in his senior year at Claflin: approximately a dozen Black students from Claflin and South Carolina State traveled to the University of South Carolina to take the Graduate Record Exam, were intercepted by a professor who told them, 'we cannot permit you to take the GRE on the campus of the University of South Carolina,' and were sent across the street (he thinks Bull Street) to a second-floor open room where they took the exam in 85-90 degree heat. After he told his father this story, his father refused to root for the University of South Carolina ever again and adopted Clemson (then an agricultural experiment station) as his school of allegiance.
related_events: GRE testing segregation incident at University of South Carolina

improvements: Could note Gaither's uncertainty about whether the street was 'Bull Street.'

chapters.title: NAACP Youth Chapter Leadership and the Orangeburg Sit-ins
chapters.summary: Gaither describes serving as president of the Claflin College NAACP youth chapter, beginning with 25-30 members out of fewer than 200 students. He recounts that after the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins and the early Rock Hill sit-ins, the Claflin chapter expanded to include almost everyone on campus, with a few students declining out of fear of reprisals against their families. He attributes the Orangeburg organizing model to CORE field secretary James T. McCain of Sumter, who brought CORE Rules for Action and James Peck's Cracking the Color Line; he also credits the inspiration of Dr. King's Stride Toward Freedom. He describes the joint Claflin–South Carolina State organizing committee with one representative from each school (he and possibly Chuck McDew of South Carolina State), the use of 'sociodramas' to rehearse confrontation, and the training of about a dozen secondary student leaders, possibly including James E. Clyburn. He notes the structural asymmetry between private Claflin (where organizing was possible) and state-funded South Carolina State (where students could face expulsion); women students faced 7:00 / 7:30 dormitory curfews that limited their direct participation. He describes the mass arrest day of approximately 350 demonstrators that overwhelmed the city jail, county jail, and an open-air stockade, and recounts his own arrest while walking around the courthouse perimeter talking to NAACP National Youth Secretary Herbert Wright. He met Matthew J. Perry Jr. that evening at the Claflin gymnasium when the attorney offered NAACP legal defense.
related_events: James Peck, Cracking the Color Line

improvements: Gaither is appropriately uncertain about Chuck McDew's identity as the South Carolina State counterpart; summary preserves that hedge.
Gaither qualifies James Clyburn's involvement as 'I think' — summary preserves the hedge.

chapters.title: Nonviolence as Tactic vs. Philosophy; James T. McCain as Mentor; Family Response
chapters.summary: Gaither describes his initial skepticism toward nonviolence when Glenn Smiley of the Fellowship of Reconciliation lectured at Claflin (Gaither's initial reaction was that 'we have been nonviolent too long'), and how James T. McCain's tactical training of CORE methodology brought him to embrace it as a technique before reading Louis Fischer's biography of Gandhi exposed him to the deeper philosophy. He distinguishes tactical nonviolence from nonviolence as a way of life, noting that on the Rock Hill road gang he often asked himself how Gandhi would behave but recognized practical limits. He characterizes McCain as low-key, committed to the cause rather than personal advancement, and his primary mentor in the Movement. Gaither describes his family's reaction to his Movement activity as deeply supportive on a religious basis ('the Lord will take care of him'), and recounts that while he was on the Rock Hill road gang a cross was burned on his parents' lawn; his father told him that if he had seen the perpetrator 'it would have been the last cross that he would have burned,' marking a generational difference in his father's commitment to family protection.
related_events: Cross burning at Gaither family home

improvements: Could include Gaither's reflection on how he 'had grown in nonviolence' from his father's generational position.

chapters.title: CORE Action Institute in Miami, Hollywood Florida Arrest, and Joining CORE Staff
chapters.summary: Gaither describes his first impressions of CORE at the national level when he visited the New York office and met executive secretary James R. 'Jim' Robinson, Jimmy McDonald, Gordon Carey, and Marvin Rich — most of the staff at that time was white aside from McCain. He recounts post-graduation voter-registration work in South Carolina with Frank Robinson of Sumter and McCain, then his attendance at the CORE Action Institute in Miami in summer 1960. There he was arrested in Hollywood, Florida while seated at a restaurant table with Dottie Miller (later briefly married to Bob Zellner); the group, which also included Bernard Lafayette, was charged with 'ejection of undesirable guests' and spent 10-12 days in the Dade County jail. The judge issued no adjudication but reserved the right to put them on probation. Gaither describes the cellblock hierarchy and a beating administered by an 'underling' to enforce a shower order on a newly-arrived elderly prisoner, and the improvised hot-chocolate technique using a Mars or Milky Way bar against an incandescent lamp. He then accepted a CORE field-secretary assignment, with early posts in Kentucky, California, and Arizona before he received the Jackson, Mississippi assignment because of his role in conceiving the Freedom Ride and scouting its route.
related_events: Hollywood, Florida arrest ('ejection of undesirable guests')
CORE national office in New York

improvements: Could elaborate on Gaither's CORE-Rules-for-Action methodology of testing restaurants with white-black pairs and observer-pairs.

chapters.title: The Friendship Nine: Jail-No-Bail in Rock Hill (January 31, 1961)
chapters.summary: Gaither describes the December 1960 training at Claflin of the Friendship Junior College students who would become the Friendship Nine, with Gordon Carey and James T. McCain involved and the same sociodrama methodology used in Orangeburg. He explains that the jail-no-bail commitment was a deliberate escalation from the earlier sit-in pattern of arrest-and-bond, designed to provide a stronger 'witness' that would reach the moral conscience of the country. The action was deliberately set for January 31, 1961 — one day before the anniversary of the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins — so the Friendship students could complete semester registration and not lose a whole semester to the 30 days on the road gang. Gaither acknowledges earlier CORE-affiliated jail-no-bail actions (Patricia Stephens and Priscilla Stephens in Tallahassee in spring 1960) but argues that the Friendship Nine had the strategic context that gave the tactic its lasting historical visibility, citing a comparison to the limited reach of Clara Luper's Oklahoma City NAACP youth sit-ins. He notes that as 'the adult sort of in that group' (the group's older mentor), he was responsible for adapting Gandhian methodology to the practical limits of the road gang.
related_events: Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Chapter sit-ins

improvements: Could elaborate on Gaither's observation that the road gang prisoners could see only a line of about a hundred visitors at a time despite a thousand-person crowd.

chapters.title: Conceiving the Freedom Ride; Scouting the Route; Montgomery Under Martial Law
chapters.summary: Gaither describes the bus ride from South Carolina to New York in December 1960 during which he and Gordon Carey, while discussing Louis Fischer's Gandhi book and the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation (scouted by George Houser), came up with the idea of a 'Freedom Ride' — Gaither says he is unsure whether he or Carey first uttered the name. The 1961 ride would extend the 1947 model into the Deep South (Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana), where Gaither anticipated federal protection would be needed; the route was chosen around historically Black colleges that could house riders and host community mass meetings. Gaither served as the scout. After the Anniston bus burning, Gaither was assigned to Montgomery, Alabama, where he stayed in Ralph Abernathy's home during the period of martial law following the mass white attack outside Abernathy's church. He describes a precise National Guard escort logistic: a Jeep with a Guardsman driver and a rear-seat Guardsman with a rifle would escort his vehicle to within a block of the Trailways or Greyhound station and then disappear; he would identify arriving Freedom Riders by waiting near a telephone booth and watching for someone to dial Abernathy's number, then escort them back through the 'red necks' to the bus station with no Guard support; the Jeep would intercept again to take them home. Gaither was then reassigned to Jackson, Mississippi as the post-arrest coordinator for incoming riders.
related_events: Montgomery mass attack and martial law (May 1961)

improvements: Gaither says 'I don't really know whether Gordon said Freedom Ride or I said Freedom Ride' — the summary preserves this attribution hedge.

chapters.title: Federal Government Ambivalence; COFO; Bob Moses; Draft Deferment and Graduate School
chapters.summary: Gaither offers a sharp critique of federal-government posture during the Movement: he was critical of the FBI (which he says he could not trust to speak frankly with, and one agent in Jackson attempted to implicate him in a conspiracy), and characterized the Kennedys' involvement as political calculation rather than moral commitment. He credits the Justice Department lawyer John Doar as a sincere and welcome presence, particularly on voter registration. He describes COFO, the Council of Federated Organizations, as encompassing SNCC, SCLC, NAACP, and CORE, organized around the recognition that the segregationist enemy made no distinctions among them; he speaks with respect for Bob Moses as a visionary who managed political-candidate campaigns for local ministers running for Congress and who, with Dave Dennis, prioritized the issues over individual prominence. Gaither then describes how, in summer 1963, he was reclassified 1A and ordered to report to Fort Jackson, South Carolina for induction; on day two the sergeant noted his arrest record and declared him not 'morally fit' to serve, releasing him to consider his options. Gaither hastily resurrected graduate-school applications and entered Atlanta University, beginning a master's degree in biology that summer/fall and continuing to PhD work at the University of Iowa. He spent the summer of 1963 with Aaron E. Henry in Clarksdale, Mississippi working on the downtown selective-buying boycott and running a Freedom School blocks from Aaron Henry's drugstore; he chose to stay in Clarksdale rather than attend the March on Washington and helped letter the 'March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom' sign that Clarksdale students carried to the march.
related_events: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom (August 28, 1963)

improvements: Could include Gaither's note that the local Jackson Black lawyers (Jack H. Young Sr., R. Jess Brown, Carsie Hall) tried most freedom-rider cases, with Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, Derrick Bell, and Carl Rachlin brought in for federal cases.

chapters.title: PhD at Iowa, Career in Electron Microscopy, and Reflections on the Freedom Rides Legacy
chapters.summary: Gaither describes his PhD work at the University of Iowa as the only African American student in his biology department, his graduate-teaching-assistant role in a large introductory biology course, and his rationale for choosing Iowa over Texas (Austin) despite the field's leading scholar being at Texas — he did not want to fight segregation in his daily research life. His specialty became electron microscopy applied to small reproductive structures (he uses the Latin etymology 'capillus, meaning a thread') and the use of improved resolution to make speciation questions objective. He then offers an extended defense of CORE's primacy in the Freedom Rides against narratives that overstate the Nashville movement's contribution: he credits the Nashville students for continuing the Rides after the Anniston bus burning when CORE riders were physically incapacitated, but rejects framings that treat CORE as 'rookies' rather than as the originators and primary sponsors. He notes he has only briefly returned to the South for visits, characterizes contemporary Southern racism as the same infrastructure with new labels, and praises the Justice Department program administration only sparingly. He expresses qualified optimism that nonviolent direct action remains viable in America given residual moral conscience.
related_events: Nashville student continuation of Freedom Rides
CORE's primary sponsorship of 1961 Freedom Rides

improvements: Could note Gaither's specific framing that the Nashville contribution was 'a part of an ongoing process, not a contribution that should consume the origin.'

chapters.title: Personal Reflections: Obama, Great Falls Today, and the Loneliness of the Black Scholar
chapters.summary: Gaither expresses surprise at Barack Obama's 2008 election given the racial baseline of the 1950s-1970s he knew, and laments that the young voters who turned out for Obama have not persisted in sending cooperative legislators to Congress, leaving Obama unable to govern against systematic obstruction. He reflects on what could have been done with the time and energy he put into 'a movement to be accepted as a citizen' if he had been born white, calling the loss 'galling.' Asked about Great Falls today, he describes a community of men his age 'biting the time away' under a tree with no industry, no well-trained labor force, no hope for their children, and 'a fundamental disconnect with the mainstream of American society.' He notes the South's effort to keep Black people down has also cheated poor white people. He closes with a reflection on the loneliness of being a Black scholar — even his parents could not fully understand his PhD in botany — and frames his contribution as being 'to the nation as a whole,' not only to Black people. The interview ends with Mosnier and Bishop's thanks for hosting them in Prospect, Pennsylvania.

improvements: Could elaborate on Gaither's lament that 'their children in many instances have been lost too.'

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: CORE Field Secretary; Freedom Ride Co-Conceiver; Biology Professor
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Chronological arc from family origins through college organizing through CORE to graduate school: 'My parents were Walter B. Gaither and Fannie M. Little Gator.' (Segment 11, 00:01:23,000)
Logical progression to professional career closure: 'After college, I had received a number of deferments.' (Segment 636, 01:41:41,480)
Reflective coda: 'I see my contribution as being more than to black people, but being to the nation as a whole.' (Segment 796, 02:10:20,480)
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a clear chronological arc — family origins, education at Claflin, NAACP youth chapter leadership, CORE Action Institute, Friendship Nine training, Freedom Ride conception, post-Movement graduate school, and contemporary reflection. Mosnier's questions facilitate orderly transitions, and Gaither's narrative threads remain coherent across topical breaks for refreshment. Minor backtracking occurs around the COFO and law-school threads.

most_vivid_moment.quote: the lid to the heater would start floating up and down and the whole place would be smoked up. And then we would have to open the windows.

evidence: Hot-chocolate-from-Mars-bar improvisation in Dade County jail: 'they would take a Mars candy bar or Milky Way. And they would put it right up next to the incandescent lamp, a light.' (Segment 432, 01:09:00,480)

National Guard escort logistic in Montgomery: 'right outside of Ralph Abernathy's house, about a half a block up, there was a Jeep with a National Gargement driving and another National Gargement in the back seat with a rifle across.' (Segment 580, 01:29:00,480)
Father's 1939 Buick used as a construction truck: 'he would load that 39 Buick with bricks and blocks and mortar and so forth' (Segment 100, 00:13:35,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Gaither offers exceptional specificity throughout — full names of more than thirty-five movement figures, precise dates including January 31, 1961 and February 1, 1960, named places (Hollywood Florida, Dade County jail, Rock Hill, Anniston, Montgomery, Jackson), and several reconstructed scenes (the GRE off-campus exam in a 90-degree second-story room, the cellblock beating, the Jeep escort logistics).

most_authentic_moment.quote: there are times when I wonder, what could I have done with that time to do something else that is automatically forthcoming if you happen to be born in this country and your skin happens to be white instead of brown? That gals me on occasion.

vulnerability_elements: Admits initial naive skepticism toward nonviolence as a tactic
Discloses 'galling' regret about the time cost of fighting for citizenship
Discloses loneliness of being a Black scholar whose work his parents could not understand
emotional_authenticity.justification: Gaither is more analytically inclined than emotionally raw, but he offers several authentic moments of vulnerability — the regret about energy lost to fighting for citizenship, the loneliness of his scholarly path, and the generational gap with his father over the cross-burning. The interview tone is reflective and measured throughout, with intellectual access to feeling rather than raw emotional release.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Gaither's narrative is exceptionally well-structured chronologically and rich in vivid, specific detail. He uses full names, precise dates, and named places throughout. The emotional register is reflective and analytical rather than raw, but he offers genuine moments of vulnerability about the personal cost of his Movement years and the loneliness of being a Black scholar.

major_events: Conception of the 1961 Freedom Ride on a bus from South Carolina to New York with Gordon Carey, December 1960 (Segment 524, 01:33:33,000)
Scout for the Freedom Ride route through historically Black colleges in the Deep South (Segment 539, 01:22:32,480)
Training of the Friendship Nine for the January 31, 1961 Rock Hill jail-no-bail action (Segment 463, 01:11:49,480)
Jackson, Mississippi post-arrest coordinator for incoming Freedom Riders (Segment 573, 01:28:00,480)
Orangeburg sit-in mass arrest of approximately 350 demonstrators (Segment 267, 00:40:51,480)

key_figures.name: James T. McCain
key_figures.relationship: Mentor and recruiter; CORE field secretary who brought Gaither into the Movement

key_figures.name: Gordon Carey
key_figures.relationship: Co-conceiver of the Freedom Ride on the December 1960 bus from South Carolina to New York

key_figures.name: James Farmer
key_figures.relationship: CORE executive secretary during the Freedom Rides; supervised Gaither

key_figures.name: Ralph Abernathy
key_figures.relationship: Hosted Gaither in his home in Montgomery during martial law in May 1961

key_figures.name: Aaron Henry
key_figures.relationship: Co-worker in the summer 1963 Clarksdale, Mississippi selective-buying boycott

key_figures.name: Bob Moses
key_figures.relationship: Met in Jackson during Freedom Rides; described as a visionary

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Gaither is one of the very few surviving direct conceivers of the 1961 Freedom Ride and the principal trainer of the Friendship Nine — both foundational events of the early Movement.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Gaither's testimony places him at the origin point of two nationally significant protests of 1961 and as a working participant in the Orangeburg sit-ins, the CORE Action Institute, Montgomery during martial law, and the Jackson rider reception. Few primary-source witnesses to these events remain.

precision_examples: Date specificity: 'January 31, 1961' as deliberate choice rather than February 1 anniversary (Segment 488, 01:14:33,480)
Numbers: '350 people arrested' at Orangeburg, '$75 per year' scholarship, '10-12 days' in Dade County jail (Segments 267, 109, 304)
Named tactical literature: 'CORE Rules for Action' and 'Cracking the Color Line by James Peck' (Segment 242, 00:34:13,480)
Named lawyers: 'Jack H. Young Sr ... R. Jess Brown ... Carsie Hall' as Jackson local counsel (Segments 662-664)
Named Freedom Ride scout precedent: 'George Houser was the scout on' the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation (Segment 531, 01:21:24,480)
verifiable_claims: Friendship Nine action on January 31, 1961 at Rock Hill, SC
Freedom Ride conceived December 1960 by Gaither and Gordon Carey
Orangeburg mass arrest of approximately 350 demonstrators
Aaron E. Henry's Clarksdale selective-buying boycott, summer 1963
specificity_of_details.justification: Specificity is high throughout — full names, dates, organizations, monetary amounts, and named documents. A historian could reconstruct most events from this transcript alone. Some scout-route details are summarized rather than enumerated, but the key claims are verifiable.

new_information: Detailed origin story of the Freedom Ride concept on a December 1960 bus from South Carolina to New York with Gordon Carey
Strategic rationale for the January 31 date for Friendship Nine (allowing students to complete semester registration)
Montgomery National Guard escort logistics during martial law (Jeep escorts that disappeared a block from the bus station)
Defense of CORE's primacy in the Freedom Rides against the Nashville-centric revisionism
understudied_aspects: Structural differences between private (Claflin) and state (South Carolina State) college organizing
Role of historically Black colleges as housing-and-meeting infrastructure for the Freedom Ride route
Trajectory from Movement organizing to academic biology career
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Gaither's account complicates the dominant Nashville-led narrative of the Freedom Rides and recenters CORE's institutional role; he also complicates a simplified federal-government-as-ally narrative by distinguishing the Justice Department's John Doar from FBI and Kennedy political calculation.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians could use this interview as primary evidence for CORE's organizational primacy in the 1961 rides, for the deliberate institutional design of the Friendship Nine jail-no-bail action, and for the local-attorney infrastructure (Young, Brown, Hall) that handled most rider cases in Jackson.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: The interview makes substantial contributions on three fronts: it documents the origin moment of the Freedom Ride concept from a participant who would otherwise be lost, it preserves the institutional rationale of the Friendship Nine action's timing, and it pushes back on revisionist accounts that subordinate CORE to the Nashville movement.

historical_value.overall_assessment: This interview is of exceptional historical value. Gaither is a primary-source witness to two foundational events of 1961 (the Friendship Nine and the Freedom Rides) and offers the only-living account of several specific institutional decisions. The specificity of names, dates, and organizations is exceptional, and the interview contains material that directly complicates dominant Movement narratives.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Gaither's arc moves from a skeptical young Black Southerner who initially rejected nonviolence as 'we have been nonviolent too long,' through tactical acceptance under James T. McCain's mentorship, into a Gandhian who internalized nonviolence as a way of life on the Rock Hill road gang, and finally into a reflective scholar weighing the personal costs of his Movement years.
key_turning_points: Initial rejection of nonviolence at Glenn Smiley's Claflin lecture: 'we have been nonviolent too long' (Segment 287, 00:44:47,480)
Tactical acceptance via James T. McCain's training and CORE Rules for Action (Segment 289, 00:45:15,480)
Gandhian internalization on the Rock Hill road gang asking 'how would Gandhi behave in this situation?' (Segment 323, 00:51:38,480)
Father's cross-burning response reveals the generational nonviolence gap (Segment 343, 00:55:04,480)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: From a rural South Carolina youth whose initial reaction to nonviolence was contempt for Black passivity, to a movement organizer who reasoned about Gandhian discipline in real time on a chain gang, to a reflective academic who measures the personal cost of having to fight for citizenship.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Gaither's reflective stance — that his cost in time and energy was 'galling' but his contribution was 'to the nation as a whole' — is inspirational in a measured, intellectual way rather than a rousing one.
transformation_growth.justification: Gaither articulates a clear philosophical transformation arc, especially on his evolving understanding of nonviolence, but does not dwell on internal emotional transformation as deeply as the rubric's top-tier expects.

peak_moments.description: Orangeburg mass arrest of approximately 350 demonstrators overwhelming the city jail, county jail, and an open-air stockade
peak_moments.stakes: first major mass-arrest action in South Carolina; risk of expulsion and physical reprisal

peak_moments.description: Hollywood, Florida arrest at the CORE Action Institute and 10-12 days in Dade County jail
peak_moments.stakes: incarceration with general-population prisoners including murderers; uncertain charge resolution

peak_moments.description: Cross-burning at Gaither's parents' home while he is on the Rock Hill road gang
peak_moments.stakes: physical danger to family; generational confrontation over violent retaliation

peak_moments.description: Montgomery National Guard escort logistic during martial law — the Guard would disappear a block from the bus station leaving Gaither and his driver to navigate 'past all of the red necks' to pick up arriving Freedom Riders
peak_moments.stakes: imminent physical violence; no police protection

conflict_types: Direct physical confrontation with white mob violence
Institutional confrontation with segregated state authority
Generational tension within Black family about violent retaliation
Intra-movement tactical conflict between NAACP legal strategy and CORE direct action
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From measured analytic detachment to indignation about FBI complicity and federal political calculation
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Gaither describes multiple concrete high-stakes moments — mass arrest, jail with murderers, cross burning, mob-adjacent National Guard escorts — but his telling tends to render the stakes through factual detail rather than dramatic peaks. The drama is in the situations themselves, not in his vocal cadence.

universal_themes: Generational gap on how to respond to injustice
Loneliness of being the 'first' in a community
Regret over time and energy spent fighting for what should be a birthright
relatable_struggles: Working through college while supporting a family (father's brickmasonry)
Choosing between vocation (biology) and Movement-aligned career (law)
Loneliness of being a Black scholar in a community that cannot understand the work
human_moments: Father's reaction to the cross-burning: 'if I had seen him, he said it would have been the last cross that he would have burned' (Segment 343, 00:55:04,480)
Father refuses to root for University of South Carolina forever after his son's GRE incident (Segment 206, 00:27:51,480)
Mother packing children's water jars with adhesive-taped names for the one-room school (Segment 30, 00:04:31,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences across backgrounds will recognize the parental sacrifice arc, the strain of being a 'first' in a profession, and the moral question about the cost of being born into a country that did not yet recognize one's citizenship.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview is rich in family-based universal experiences (the cross-burning, the father's allegiance switch, the mother's school improvisations) that resonate broadly while remaining specifically situated in Black Southern experience under segregation.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Gaither's interview offers strong emotional resonance through specific family moments and the philosophical journey from skeptical youth to committed Gandhian to reflective scholar. The emotional register is restrained and analytical rather than raw, but multiple universally human moments anchor the story.

barriers: Movement-specific acronyms (CORE, SNCC, SCLC, COFO, NAACP, GRE) used frequently; some are self-defined, some are not
Late-career biological terms (electron microscopy, capillus, speciation, resolution) used with brief but technical glosses
language_clarity.justification: Gaither is an articulate former professor and a clear, well-organized speaker. He self-defines most Movement acronyms (he expands COFO when prompted by Mosnier) and offers etymological glosses for his scientific terms. Only the most technical electron-microscopy passage may slow general listeners.

high_energy_sections: Friendship Nine and Freedom Ride conception passages (Segments 369-535, 01:01:04 - 01:21:43): vivid recall and forward narrative momentum
Hollywood, Florida arrest and Dade County jail scenes (Segments 299-435, 00:47:55 - 01:09:12): well-paced anecdotal storytelling
slow_sections: Electron microscopy explanation (Segments 720-732, 01:54:52 - 01:57:03): slower technical exposition
Pleasant Grove one-room-school environmental description (Segments 30-45, 00:04:31 - 00:06:26): detailed but slow-paced background
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: General audiences should stay engaged through most of the two-hour interview; the post-Movement biology section may lose listeners not interested in academic biography.
narrative_flow.justification: The interview maintains good pacing for the first 1:50 with vivid anecdote and forward chronological motion. The graduate-school and electron-microscopy passages are slower, and the late reflective sections recover momentum but at a more measured pace. Mosnier's questions consistently support flow.

themes_connected: Voter ID resurgence framed as 'a throwback' to the qualified-elector certificate (Segment 170, 00:24:02,480)
Comparison of Obama-era political obstruction to early-Movement federal political calculation
Persistent infrastructure of Southern racism under new labels
Loss to the nation when potential is wasted on fighting for citizenship by birth
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Gaither explicitly connects 1960s voter suppression mechanics to contemporary voter-ID laws and the 1960s federal-political-calculation pattern to the obstruction Obama faced.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Gaither consistently helps audiences see significance — he explicitly explains the strategic rationale for the Friendship Nine date, the institutional design of the Freedom Ride route, and the larger meaning of CORE's role.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Gaither is a born teacher; he consistently frames specific experiences in larger institutional and contemporary context, making the 'so what?' explicit throughout.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Gaither is a highly accessible speaker for a general audience, with clear articulation, deliberate self-explanation of acronyms and concepts, and explicit framing of contemporary relevance. The slower passages on graduate-school work and electron microscopy do not undermine accessibility for the rest of the interview.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is an exceptional Civil Rights History Project interview from a foundational organizer of the Freedom Rides and the Friendship Nine. Gaither offers high-precision testimony with full names, exact dates, and named documents throughout, while complicating the dominant Nashville-centric Freedom Rides narrative through direct primary-source authority. His narrative arc — from one-room South Carolina schoolhouse to CORE field secretary to biology professor — is well-structured and rich in family detail. The emotional register is reflective and analytical rather than raw, but moments of genuine vulnerability (the regret about lost time, the loneliness of being a Black scholar) ground the interview in authentic human experience.
key_strengths: Primary-source authority: Gaither is one of the very few surviving witnesses who can speak directly to the conception of the 1961 Freedom Ride and the institutional design of the Friendship Nine jail-no-bail action — both nationally foundational events.

Specificity: full names of more than thirty-five Movement figures, precise dates including January 31, 1961, named tactical literature (CORE Rules for Action, Cracking the Color Line), named lawyers (Young, Brown, Hall, Motley, Kunstler), and named institutional infrastructure (Claflin chapter, CORE national office, COFO).

Explicit historical revision: Gaither pushes back against the Nashville-centric Freedom Rides narrative with specific arguments rooted in CORE's role as originator and primary sponsor — a contribution unavailable in most secondary literature.
key_limitations: Reflective rather than raw emotional register may make this interview less immediately engaging for audiences seeking dramatic moments of breakdown or catharsis.

Several attribution hedges ('I think it was', 'I'm not absolutely certain') leave specific points (Chuck McDew's role, the Bull Street name, Clyburn's participation) appropriately uncertain but slow specific verification.

claims.claim: Gaither was born in Great Falls, South Carolina to Walter B. Gaither and Fannie Mae Little Gaither, both Friendship Junior College graduates.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My parents were Walter B. Gaither and Fannie M. Little Gator ... my mom and dad met while they were students at Friendship Junior College in Rock Hill, South Carolina (segments 11-13)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes parents' names and Friendship Junior College attendance. 'Gator' is an ASR variant of Gaither.

claims.claim: Gaither's father was terminated from teaching after confronting the white school-board official about a five-dollar payroll discrepancy.
claims.supporting_excerpt: for questioning the five dollar differential which the school board member was pocketing, my father was terminated as a teacher (segment 21)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the cause and termination.

claims.claim: Gaither's mother taught him for his first seven years at Pleasant Grove AME Zion church school.
claims.supporting_excerpt: my first seven years of formal education was in a one room school ... So my and happened to be Pleasant Grove school. Pleasant Grove was a nearby AME Zion church (segments 22-26)
claims.rationale: Transcript establishes name, denomination, and length of schooling.

claims.claim: Gaither's father returned to Benedict College in Columbia and graduated with a Bachelor of Science in sociology.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He went back to college at Benedict College in Columbia, South Carolina ... he completed the other two years of his college degree graduating with a Bachelor of Science degree in sociology (segments 91-92)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the institution, completion, and degree.

claims.claim: Gaither attended Claflin College in Orangeburg, South Carolina with his brother starting in 1956.
claims.supporting_excerpt: we jointly decided that we would go to Claflin College now Claflin College University in Orangeburg, South Carolina (segment 108) ... Tell me about Orange Bird when you arrived in 56 (segment 176)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Claflin, Orangeburg, the brother's joint attendance, and the 1956 arrival date (Mosnier's question confirmed by Gaither).

claims.claim: Gaither served as president of the Claflin College NAACP youth chapter.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was the president of the youth chapter of the Claflin College chapter of the NAACP (segment 220)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the leadership role.

claims.claim: Discussions of the 1955 Emmett Till case at Gaither's family dinner table were a motivating factor in his later Movement participation.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I especially remember the till case utterings by my dad and the absolute shame and horror of what had happened to this young man ... conversations about till that, large in my psyche as one of the motivating factors for participating in the movement later on. Till would have been 1955 (segments 118-120)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Till as a discussion topic, 1955 as the date, and Gaither's framing of it as a motivating factor.

claims.claim: CORE field secretary James T. McCain of Sumter mentored Gaither and brought CORE Rules for Action and James Peck's Cracking the Color Line.
claims.supporting_excerpt: James T. McCain who was from Sumter, South Carolina and he worked for an organization called the Congress of Rachel E. Quality, a Corps. And Corps had a little thing that was called CORE Rules for Action. It was also a little booklet called Cracking the Color Line by James Peck (segments 241-242)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes McCain's affiliation, the documents, and the authorship of the Peck booklet.

claims.claim: The Orangeburg mass arrest involved approximately 350 demonstrators, overwhelming the city jail, county jail, and an open-air stockade.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The day in Argeburg, when we had a mass arrest, 350 people arrested ... City jail filled, County jail filled, open air stockade (segments 267-268)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the 350 figure and the three holding facilities.

claims.claim: Gaither met Matthew J. Perry Jr. at the Claflin gymnasium the evening of the mass arrest.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I remembered that we met back up at the gymnasium on the campus of Claflin College. And that was the first time that I remember meeting Matthew J. Perry Jr, who just died recently (segments 278-279)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the gymnasium meeting and Perry's identification.

claims.claim: Gaither attended the CORE Action Institute in Miami in summer 1960 and was arrested in Hollywood, Florida.
claims.supporting_excerpt: CORE held at Miami, what was called an (CORE) Action Institute ... I was sitting at a table in a little community that is called Hollywood ... Hollywood, Florida, yes (segments 298-300)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the Miami institute, Hollywood Florida location, and arrest.

claims.claim: Gaither was arrested with Dottie Miller, who was briefly married to Bob Zellner.
claims.supporting_excerpt: with a girl from New York named Dottie Miller. Doddy later was for a short time married to Bob Zellner (segments 300-301)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Miller's name and her short marriage to Zellner.

claims.claim: The Hollywood, Florida charge was 'ejection of undesirable guests' and resulted in 10-12 days in Dade County jail.
claims.supporting_excerpt: we spent 10 or 12 days in the date, county jail ... The charge was ejection of undesirable guests (segments 304-305)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the charge phrase and jail duration.

claims.claim: Gaither and Gordon Carey conceived the Freedom Ride on a December 1960 bus from South Carolina to New York while discussing Louis Fischer's Gandhi book.
claims.supporting_excerpt: we were going from South Carolina after a training session back to New York. I think Gordon had a copy of Louis Fischer's book about Gandhi ... And I came up with the idea, it broke the idea of a Freedom Ride (segments 526-529)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the bus trip, the Carey companionship, Fischer's book, and the conception. Gaither hedges on who first uttered the name.

claims.claim: The Freedom Ride route was patterned on the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation, scouted by George Houser.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The Freedom Riders would essentially copy some of the basic pattern of a 1947 journey that had been staged by the Fellowship of Reconciliation and Corps jointly. That's the ride that George Houser was the scout on (segments 530-531)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the 1947 precedent, the FOR/CORE joint sponsorship, and Houser as the scout.

claims.claim: After the Anniston bus burning, Gaither was assigned to Montgomery, Alabama and stayed in Ralph Abernathy's home during martial law.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My assignment right after the bus was burned in Anniston was in Montgomery, Alabama. And in Montgomery, I lived in the home of Ralph Abernathy ... Montgomery was under martial law (segments 574-575)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Anniston, the Montgomery assignment, Abernathy as host, and martial law.

claims.claim: The Friendship Nine jail-no-bail action was deliberately scheduled for January 31, 1961 (one day before the February 1 Greensboro anniversary) to let students complete semester registration.
claims.supporting_excerpt: the students were interested in being able to register for the next semester of classes ... we decided to have the arrest on the 31st where they could accomplish that rather than putting it so that it was February 1st (segments 481, 488)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the date, the rationale, and the contrast with the anniversary date.

claims.claim: Earlier CORE-affiliated jail-no-bail actions by Patricia Stephens and Priscilla Stephens occurred in Tallahassee, Florida.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I would never detract one iota from the significance of what happened to this core group in Tallahassee, Patricia Stephens (Due), Priscilla Stephens, and those people (segment 387)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the Tallahassee location, CORE affiliation, and the Stephens sisters by name.

claims.claim: Gaither worked with Aaron E. Henry in Clarksdale, Mississippi in summer 1963 on a downtown selective-buying boycott.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I worked with Aaron E. Henry in Clarksdale, Mississippi Mississippi. And Aaron E. Henry and the Clarksdale, Mississippi NAACP were involved in a selective buying program, actually a boycott, of downtown Clarksdale, Mississippi (segments 669-670)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the Clarksdale assignment, Henry's role, and the boycott.

claims.claim: Gaither chose to stay in Clarksdale rather than attend the March on Washington and helped letter the 'March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom' sign that Clarksdale students carried.
claims.supporting_excerpt: my choice was to stay in Clarksdale, Mississippi or to go to the March on Washington. And I opted to stay in Clarksdale, Mississippi. And I remember as I told you there, helping to put together the sign March on Washington for jobs in freedom (segments 674-675)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the choice, the location, and the sign-lettering.

claims.claim: COFO encompassed SNCC, SCLC, NAACP, and CORE.
claims.supporting_excerpt: COFO was the Council of Federated Organizations. And the representative organizations were SNCC ... SCLC ... NAACP ... and core (segments 686-687)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the four-organization composition.

claims.claim: Gaither was ordered to report to Fort Jackson, South Carolina for induction; the sergeant declared him not 'morally fit' to serve due to his arrest record, and he then entered Atlanta University graduate school in biology.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I received a notice that I had to report to Fort Jackson, South Carolina for induction into the army ... I see you have an arrest record here ... you're not morally fit to serve in the military ... So I found myself in graduate school rather quickly (segments 637-644)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Fort Jackson, the 'morally fit' phrase, and the Atlanta University entry.

claims.claim: Gaither earned his PhD at the University of Iowa, specializing in electron microscopy.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I chose the one at the University of Iowa (segment 654) ... eventually I got into an area of biology that's called electron microscopy (segment 720)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Iowa as the PhD institution and electron microscopy as the specialty.

claims.claim: Gaither served on a Rock Hill road gang for the Friendship Nine action and a cross was burned on his parents' lawn during that time.
claims.supporting_excerpt: While your son is 29 miles away on the road game ... while I was in jail in Rock Hill, a cross was burned on the lawn of my parents' house (segments 341-342)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes both the road gang and the cross-burning.

claims.claim: Glenn Smiley of the Fellowship of Reconciliation gave the lecture at Claflin where Gaither first encountered the philosophy of nonviolence.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The first time I heard someone talk about nonviolence was man by the name of Glenn Smiley, who worked for the Fellowship of Reconciliation. He gave a lecture at Claflin talking about nonviolence (segments 286-287)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes Smiley's name, FOR affiliation, the Claflin venue, and Gaither's first encounter.

claims.claim: The Jackson, Mississippi Freedom Rider cases were primarily handled by local Black lawyers Jack H. Young Sr., R. Jess Brown, and Carsie Hall, with federal cases involving Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, Derrick Bell, and Carl Rachlin.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was Jack H. Young Sr ... R. Jess Brown ... another man named Carsie Hall ... if it was a federal case, we would get people like Derrick Bell and Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, or Carl Rachlin (segments 662-665)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes all named attorneys and the local/federal division of labor.

claims.claim: Bob Moses was involved in managing campaigns of local Mississippi ministers running for Congress when Gaither first met him in Jackson.
claims.supporting_excerpt: When we were focused on taking care of Freedom Riders in Jackson, which is when I first met Bob Moses, Bob Moses was involved in managing the campaigns of some of the local ministry. The local ministers who were running for Congress (segments 631-632)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes the Jackson meeting and the campaign-management role.

claims.claim: Gaither was interviewed September 12, 2011 in Prospect, Pennsylvania by Joseph Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with videographer John Bishop.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Today is Monday, September 12, 2011. My name is Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill ... I am with videographer John Bishop. We are in Prospect, Pennsylvania today to complete an interview (segments 2-3)
claims.rationale: Transcript directly establishes all interview-metadata fields.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary scores 92 accuracy / 91 quality, both above the Smithsonian-grade 90/90 threshold. The per-claim citation audit identifies 28 factual claims and finds all 28 directly supported by specific transcript segments, with zero partially-supported and zero unsupported claims. The interview is also corroborated by 16 named figures cross-checked against the ground-truth corpus (Thomas Walter Gaither, Friendship Nine, Freedom Riders, Congress of Racial Equality, Gordon Carey, James T. McCain, James Farmer, Bob Moses, Aaron Henry, Matthew J. Perry Jr., Charles F. "Chuck" McDew, Bob Zellner, Ralph Abernathy, Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, John Doar — all canonical spellings and roles match). The summary preserves Gaither's attribution hedges ('I think it was', 'I'm not absolutely certain') where they appear in the transcript. The interview is recommended for publication.
